revision.comment: Formatting for Korea-related articles, replaced: [[Korea under Japanese rule|Chōsen]] → [[Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]], website=파이낸셜뉴스 → website=[[The Financial News]]
1 revision imported from [[:wikipedia:Keijō_Shrine]]: importing single revision from english wikipedia to provide context instead of writing an inferior article for here

Moving [[:Category:Buildings and structures destroyed in 1945]] to [[:Category:Buildings and structures demolished in 1945]] per [[Wikipedia:Categories for discussion/Log/2024 November 7#Category:Buildings and structures by decade of destruction]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Keijō Shrine]][[en:Keijō Shrine]][[ja:京城神社]][[ko:경성신사]][[simple:Keijō Shrine]][[zh:京城神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Keijo Shrine.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|qid=Q812767}}|prefecture={{ill|Keijō|qid=Q483984}}|coordinates={{Coord|37.5569|N|126.9864|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|1898–1945 Shinto shrine in Seoul, Korea}} {{For|the Shinto shrine on Namsan|Chōsen Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg | caption = Part of the shrine complex | deity = [[Amaterasu]], {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]], [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]]? | municipality = [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] | country = [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]] | coordinates = {{coord|37.5569|126.9864|display=inline,title}} | established = {{start date|1898|11|3}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|11|17}} | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to present-day Seoul }} {{Nihongo|'''Keijō Shrine'''|京城神社|Keijō-jinja|{{Korean|hangul=경성신사}}}}, sometimes '''Seoul Shrine''',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henry |first=Todd |title=Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945 |publisher=University of California Press |year=2014 |isbn=9780520958418}}</ref>{{rp|65}}<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] (Seoul), [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]]. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>
The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].
== Theological history == Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed"> File:朝鮮神宮本殿正面、朝鮮神宮一の鳥居(京城).jpg|alt=|A building in the shrine complex dedicated to Japanese General [[Nogi Maresuke]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=정 |first=용부 |date=2018-02-02 |title=[다크 헤리티지를 찾아서] 서울미래유산에 남겨진 일제 신사의 유구 |url=https://www.fnnews.com/news/201802011646544711 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=[[The Financial News]] |language=ko-KR}}</ref> File:Keijyo shrine 10.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg|alt=|Honden File:Keijyo shrine 13.jpeg|alt=|Interior File:Keijyo shrine 14.jpeg|alt=|Pavilion File:Keijyo shrine 3.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 4.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 5.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 6.jpeg|alt=|Entrance statues </gallery>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Keijō Shrine]][[en:Keijō Shrine]][[ja:京城神社]][[ko:경성신사]][[simple:Keijō Shrine]][[zh:京城神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Keijo Shrine.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}}|prefecture={{ill|Keijō|WD=Q483984}}|coordinates={{Coord|37.5569|N|126.9864|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|1898–1945 Shinto shrine in Seoul, Korea}} {{For|the Shinto shrine on Namsan|Chōsen Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg | caption = Part of the shrine complex | deity = [[Amaterasu]], {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]], [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]]? | municipality = [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] | country = [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]] | coordinates = {{coord|37.5569|126.9864|display=inline,title}} | established = {{start date|1898|11|3}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|11|17}} | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to present-day Seoul }} {{Nihongo|'''Keijō Shrine'''|京城神社|Keijō-jinja|{{Korean|hangul=경성신사}}}}, sometimes '''Seoul Shrine''',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henry |first=Todd |title=Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945 |publisher=University of California Press |year=2014 |isbn=9780520958418}}</ref>{{rp|65}}<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] (Seoul), [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]]. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>
The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].
== Theological history == Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed"> File:朝鮮神宮本殿正面、朝鮮神宮一の鳥居(京城).jpg|alt=|A building in the shrine complex dedicated to Japanese General [[Nogi Maresuke]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=정 |first=용부 |date=2018-02-02 |title=[다크 헤리티지를 찾아서] 서울미래유산에 남겨진 일제 신사의 유구 |url=https://www.fnnews.com/news/201802011646544711 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=[[The Financial News]] |language=ko-KR}}</ref> File:Keijyo shrine 10.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg|alt=|Honden File:Keijyo shrine 13.jpeg|alt=|Interior File:Keijyo shrine 14.jpeg|alt=|Pavilion File:Keijyo shrine 3.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 4.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 5.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 6.jpeg|alt=|Entrance statues </gallery>

Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Keijō Shrine]][[en:Keijō Shrine]][[ja:京城神社]][[ko:경성신사]][[simple:Keijō Shrine]][[zh:京城神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Keijo Shrine.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}}|prefecture={{ill|Keijō|WD=Q483984}}|coordinates={{Coord|37.5569|N|126.9864|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|1898–1945 Shinto shrine in Seoul, Korea}} {{For|the Shinto shrine on Namsan|Chōsen Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg | caption = Part of the shrine complex | deity = [[Amaterasu]], {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]], [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]]? | municipality = [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] | country = [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]] | coordinates = {{coord|37.5569|126.9864|display=inline,title}} | established = {{start date|1898|11|3}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|11|17}} | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to present-day Seoul }} {{Nihongo|'''Keijō Shrine'''|京城神社|Keijō-jinja|{{Korean|hangul=경성신사}}}}, sometimes '''Seoul Shrine''',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henry |first=Todd |title=Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945 |publisher=University of California Press |year=2014 |isbn=9780520958418}}</ref>{{rp|65}}<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] (Seoul), [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]]. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>
The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].
== Theological history == Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed"> File:朝鮮神宮本殿正面、朝鮮神宮一の鳥居(京城).jpg|alt=|A building in the shrine complex dedicated to Japanese General [[Nogi Maresuke]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=정 |first=용부 |date=2018-02-02 |title=[다크 헤리티지를 찾아서] 서울미래유산에 남겨진 일제 신사의 유구 |url=https://www.fnnews.com/news/201802011646544711 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=[[The Financial News]] |language=ko-KR}}</ref> File:Keijyo shrine 10.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg|alt=|Honden File:Keijyo shrine 13.jpeg|alt=|Interior File:Keijyo shrine 14.jpeg|alt=|Pavilion File:Keijyo shrine 3.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 4.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 5.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 6.jpeg|alt=|Entrance statues </gallery>

{{wikidata link|Q10855232}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Keijō Shrine]][[en:Keijō Shrine]][[ja:京城神社]][[ko:경성신사]][[simple:Keijō Shrine]][[zh:京城神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Keijo Shrine.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}}|prefecture={{ill|Keijō|WD=Q483984}}|coordinates={{Coord|37.5569|N|126.9864|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|1898–1945 Shinto shrine in Seoul, Korea}} {{For|the Shinto shrine on Namsan|Chōsen Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg | caption = Part of the shrine complex | deity = [[Amaterasu]], {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]], [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]]? | municipality = [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] | country = [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]] | coordinates = {{coord|37.5569|126.9864|display=inline,title}} | established = {{start date|1898|11|3}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|11|17}} | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to present-day Seoul }} {{Nihongo|'''Keijō Shrine'''|京城神社|Keijō-jinja|{{Korean|hangul=경성신사}}}}, sometimes '''Seoul Shrine''',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henry |first=Todd |title=Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945 |publisher=University of California Press |year=2014 |isbn=9780520958418}}</ref>{{rp|65}}<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] (Seoul), [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]]. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>
The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].
== Theological history == Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}

Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] (Seoul), [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]]. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>
The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].
== Theological history == Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed"> File:朝鮮神宮本殿正面、朝鮮神宮一の鳥居(京城).jpg|alt=|A building in the shrine complex dedicated to Japanese General [[Nogi Maresuke]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=정 |first=용부 |date=2018-02-02 |title=[다크 헤리티지를 찾아서] 서울미래유산에 남겨진 일제 신사의 유구 |url=https://www.fnnews.com/news/201802011646544711 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=[[The Financial News]] |language=ko-KR}}</ref> File:Keijyo shrine 10.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg|alt=|Honden File:Keijyo shrine 13.jpeg|alt=|Interior File:Keijyo shrine 14.jpeg|alt=|Pavilion File:Keijyo shrine 3.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 4.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 5.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 6.jpeg|alt=|Entrance statues </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Keijō Shrine]][[en:Keijō Shrine]][[ja:京城神社]][[ko:경성신사]][[simple:Keijō Shrine]][[zh:京城神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Keijō Shrine]][[en:Keijō Shrine]][[ja:京城神社]][[ko:경성신사]][[simple:Keijō Shrine]][[zh:京城神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Keijō Shrine]][[en:Keijō Shrine]][[ja:京城神社]][[ko:경성신사]][[simple:Keijō Shrine]][[zh:京城神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Keijo Shrine.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}}|prefecture={{ill|Keijō|WD=Q483984}}|coordinates={{Coord|37.5569|N|126.9864|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|1898–1945 Shinto shrine in Seoul, Korea}} {{For|the Shinto shrine on Namsan|Chōsen Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg | caption = Part of the shrine complex | deity = [[Amaterasu]], {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]], [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]]? | municipality = [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] | country = [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]] | coordinates = {{coord|37.5569|126.9864|display=inline,title}} | established = {{start date|1898|11|3}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|11|17}} | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to present-day Seoul }} {{Nihongo|'''Keijō Shrine'''|京城神社|Keijō-jinja|{{Korean|hangul=경성신사}}}}, sometimes '''Seoul Shrine''',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henry |first=Todd |title=Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945 |publisher=University of California Press |year=2014 |isbn=9780520958418}}</ref>{{rp|65}}<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Keijō|Keijō]] (Seoul), [[:en:Korea under Japanese rule|Korea, Empire of Japan]]. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>
The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].
== Theological history == Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed"> File:朝鮮神宮本殿正面、朝鮮神宮一の鳥居(京城).jpg|alt=|A building in the shrine complex dedicated to Japanese General [[Nogi Maresuke]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=정 |first=용부 |date=2018-02-02 |title=[다크 헤리티지를 찾아서] 서울미래유산에 남겨진 일제 신사의 유구 |url=https://www.fnnews.com/news/201802011646544711 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=[[The Financial News]] |language=ko-KR}}</ref> File:Keijyo shrine 10.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg|alt=|Honden File:Keijyo shrine 13.jpeg|alt=|Interior File:Keijyo shrine 14.jpeg|alt=|Pavilion File:Keijyo shrine 3.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 4.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 5.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 6.jpeg|alt=|Entrance statues </gallery>

The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>
The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].
== Theological history == Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed"> File:朝鮮神宮本殿正面、朝鮮神宮一の鳥居(京城).jpg|alt=|A building in the shrine complex dedicated to Japanese General [[Nogi Maresuke]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=정 |first=용부 |date=2018-02-02 |title=[다크 헤리티지를 찾아서] 서울미래유산에 남겨진 일제 신사의 유구 |url=https://www.fnnews.com/news/201802011646544711 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=[[The Financial News]] |language=ko-KR}}</ref> File:Keijyo shrine 10.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg|alt=|Honden File:Keijyo shrine 13.jpeg|alt=|Interior File:Keijyo shrine 14.jpeg|alt=|Pavilion File:Keijyo shrine 3.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 4.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 5.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 6.jpeg|alt=|Entrance statues </gallery>

| municipality = {{ill|Keijō|en|Keijō|WD=Q483984|lt=Keijō}} | country = {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea, Empire of Japan}} | coordinates = {{coord|37.5569|126.9864|display=inline,title}} | established = {{start date|1898|11|3}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|11|17}} | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to present-day Seoul }} {{Nihongo|'''Keijō Shrine'''|京城神社|Keijō-jinja|{{Korean|hangul=경성신사}}}}, sometimes '''Seoul Shrine''',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henry |first=Todd |title=Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945 |publisher=University of California Press |year=2014 |isbn=9780520958418}}</ref>{{rp|65}}<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Keijō|en|Keijō|WD=Q483984|lt=Keijō}} (Seoul), {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea, Empire of Japan}}. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>
The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].

Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Keijō Shrine]][[en:Keijō Shrine]][[ja:京城神社]][[ko:경성신사]][[simple:Keijō Shrine]][[zh:京城神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Keijō Shrine]][[en:Keijō Shrine]][[ja:京城神社]][[ko:경성신사]][[simple:Keijō Shrine]][[zh:京城神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Keijō Shrine]][[en:Keijō Shrine]][[ja:京城神社]][[ko:경성신사]][[simple:Keijō Shrine]][[zh:京城神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Keijo Shrine.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}}|prefecture={{ill|Keijō|WD=Q483984}}|coordinates={{Coord|37.5569|N|126.9864|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855232]] Wikidata name: Keijō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|1898–1945 Shinto shrine in Seoul, Korea}} {{For|the Shinto shrine on Namsan|Chōsen Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg | caption = Part of the shrine complex | deity = [[Amaterasu]], {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]], [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]]? | municipality = {{ill|Keijō|en|Keijō|WD=Q483984|lt=Keijō}} | country = {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea, Empire of Japan}} | coordinates = {{coord|37.5569|126.9864|display=inline,title}} | established = {{start date|1898|11|3}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|11|17}} | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to present-day Seoul }} {{Nihongo|'''Keijō Shrine'''|京城神社|Keijō-jinja|{{Korean|hangul=경성신사}}}}, sometimes '''Seoul Shrine''',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henry |first=Todd |title=Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945 |publisher=University of California Press |year=2014 |isbn=9780520958418}}</ref>{{rp|65}}<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Keijō|en|Keijō|WD=Q483984|lt=Keijō}} (Seoul), {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea, Empire of Japan}}. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>
The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].
== Theological history == Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed"> File:朝鮮神宮本殿正面、朝鮮神宮一の鳥居(京城).jpg|alt=|A building in the shrine complex dedicated to Japanese General [[Nogi Maresuke]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=정 |first=용부 |date=2018-02-02 |title=[다크 헤리티지를 찾아서] 서울미래유산에 남겨진 일제 신사의 유구 |url=https://www.fnnews.com/news/201802011646544711 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=[[The Financial News]] |language=ko-KR}}</ref> File:Keijyo shrine 10.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg|alt=|Honden File:Keijyo shrine 13.jpeg|alt=|Interior File:Keijyo shrine 14.jpeg|alt=|Pavilion File:Keijyo shrine 3.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 4.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 5.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 6.jpeg|alt=|Entrance statues </gallery>

== Theological history == Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}

{{Short description|1898–1945 Shinto shrine in Seoul, Korea}} {{For|the Shinto shrine on Namsan|Chōsen Shrine}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg | caption = Part of the shrine complex | deity = [[Amaterasu]], {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]], [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]]? | municipality = {{ill|Keijō|en|Keijō|WD=Q483984|lt=Keijō|1=Keijō}} | country = {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea, Empire of Japan|1=Korea under Japanese rule}} | coordinates = {{coord|37.5569|126.9864|display=inline,title}} | established = {{start date|1898|11|3}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|11|17}} | module = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=14}}location relative to present-day Seoul }} {{Nihongo|'''Keijō Shrine'''|京城神社|Keijō-jinja|{{Korean|hangul=경성신사}}}}, sometimes '''Seoul Shrine''',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henry |first=Todd |title=Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945 |publisher=University of California Press |year=2014 |isbn=9780520958418}}</ref>{{rp|65}}<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Keijō|en|Keijō|WD=Q483984|lt=Keijō|1=Keijō}} (Seoul), {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea, Empire of Japan|1=Korea under Japanese rule}}. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>
The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].
== Theological history == Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed"> File:朝鮮神宮本殿正面、朝鮮神宮一の鳥居(京城).jpg|alt=|A building in the shrine complex dedicated to Japanese General [[Nogi Maresuke]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=정 |first=용부 |date=2018-02-02 |title=[다크 헤리티지를 찾아서] 서울미래유산에 남겨진 일제 신사의 유구 |url=https://www.fnnews.com/news/201802011646544711 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=[[The Financial News]] |language=ko-KR}}</ref> File:Keijyo shrine 10.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 11.jpeg|alt=|Honden File:Keijyo shrine 13.jpeg|alt=|Interior File:Keijyo shrine 14.jpeg|alt=|Pavilion File:Keijyo shrine 3.jpeg|alt=|Torii File:Keijyo shrine 4.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 5.jpeg|alt=| File:Keijyo shrine 6.jpeg|alt=|Entrance statues </gallery>

{{Nihongo|'''Keijō Shrine'''|京城神社|Keijō-jinja|{{Korean|hangul=경성신사}}}}, sometimes '''Seoul Shrine''',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henry |first=Todd |title=Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945 |publisher=University of California Press |year=2014 |isbn=9780520958418}}</ref>{{rp|65}}<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Keijō|en|Keijō|WD=Q483984|lt=Keijō|1=Keijō}} (Seoul), {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea, Empire of Japan|1=Korea under Japanese rule}}. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>

The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].

== Theological history ==
Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}

Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}

In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}

{{Nihongo|'''Keijō Shrine'''|京城神社|Keijō-jinja|{{Korean|hangul=경성신사}}}}, sometimes '''Seoul Shrine''',<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henry |first=Todd |title=Assimilating Seoul: Japanese Rule and the Politics of Public Space in Colonial Korea, 1910–1945 |publisher=University of California Press |year=2014 |isbn=9780520958418}}</ref>{{rp|65}}<ref name="shimizu-2022a">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|139}} was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Keijō|en|Keijō|qid=Q483984|lt=Keijō|1=Keijō}} (Seoul), {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|qid=Q503585|lt=Korea, Empire of Japan|1=Korea under Japanese rule}}. The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>

The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].

== Theological history ==
Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}

Uniquely it referred to Kunitama as Chosen Kunitama suggesting a distinctly Korean flavor, as this shrine attempted to integrate many Korean customs.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}} Many locals identified "Chosen Kunitama" with [[:en:Dangun|Dangun]].<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}

In 1936 the government released a memo saying that Okunitama was in fact a generic title for any Korean deity and not Dangun. The name was also changed to Kunitama-no-Okami as a parallel to [[Amaterasu]]<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}

The shrine was established on November 3, 1898,<ref name="青井2">青井哲人「ソウル・南山の神域化－植民都市と神社境内」（明治聖徳記念学会紀要復刊第43号、2006th year ）</ref><ref>「神社deity並創立調査表」 JACAR（アジア歴史資料センター） Ref.A03010213400 （国立公文書館）</ref> and destroyed on November 17, 1945, several months after the end of colonial rule.<ref>内務省告示第264号 官報第5660号（昭和20th year 11月22日） 1頁</ref>

The shrine was located to the north of the mountain [[Namsan (Seoul)|Namsan]].

Initially the shrine only worshipped [[Amaterasu]] but it later added the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] used in Japanese colonial shrines. after it was established that it would not become the Chosen Jingu.<ref name="shimizu-2022a"/>{{rp|140}}
